result.prose:
Something moves behind the broker's eyes. Not surprise — satisfaction. The small, professional kind. The kind that says *there it is.*

"Practical," they say. "Good. Practical is faster."

They turn the datapad around. A manifest header. Cargo classification redacted. Destination listed as Kerrivan's Reach — a frontier settlement three beacon hops out, on a corridor that's been technically functional since a partial relay restoration eight months ago. *Technically* being the operative word.

"Sealed container. Four meters by two. Standard freight dimensions." They tap the manifest. "Pickup from a Bloc intermediate at Kerrivan's Approach beacon. Delivery to a private recipient at the settlement. No inspection rights. No questions about contents. Standard runner's contract — you carry, you deliver, you collect."

They slide the datapad across the crate toward you.

"Forty thousand on delivery confirmation. The marker clears before you undock — I can have Thorane's people file the release within the hour."

The manifest is clean. Too clean. The kind of clean that comes from someone who is very good at making things look like they aren't what they are.

Kerrivan's Reach is a colony settlement. Mostly families. The kind of place that scrapes by on mutual aid and whatever the supply runners bring through the degraded corridor.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly. *"Kerrivan's Reach has a population of approximately two thousand three hundred. Mostly residential. Their medical infrastructure was damaged in the beacon corridor disruption eight months ago. They've been requesting pharmaceutical resupply through three separate freight boards for the last six weeks with no successful bids."*

A pause.

*"I note that a four-by-two sealed container with Bloc military classification markings and no inspection rights is not a standard configuration for pharmaceutical freight. I also note that I am now concerned, which is not a system state I enter lightly."*

Across the bay, Sura still hasn't moved. But the multi-tool is resting on the deck plate now. Hands empty. Listening.

The broker waits. They are very good at waiting.

The berth lock formalizes in sixteen minutes.

result.prose:
"Until the clamps formalize," the broker says. "Which is—"

*"Eight minutes and forty seconds,"* MIRA supplies. *"Give or take the Harbour Office clerk's typing speed. Fen Toussaint runs an efficient desk. I'd give or take less rather than more."*

The broker absorbs this without visible irritation, which tells you they've dealt with ship AIs before or have very good emotional regulation. Possibly both.

"Eight minutes," they confirm. "After that, the hold is official and your options narrow considerably. Deferral requires a creditor agreement Thorane won't give you without something in return, and contesting the lien through Gannett's process takes—"

"Weeks," you say.

"Three to five, yes. During which the *Endurance* sits on those clamps." They let that image settle. "I can have the marker pulled in the next seven minutes. One comm to Thorane's representative here on station. The hold never formalizes. You unclamp clean."

They mean it as a comfort. It lands as something else — a demonstration of how thoroughly this situation was assembled before you knew it existed. Thorane's representative already on Gannett. The broker already in motion. The maintenance access already mapped.

Seven minutes and change.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says, quieter now, just for you. *"Khe'Voz is asking from the engine room whether he should start pre-ignition sequence. I told him I'd ask. He says, and I'm quoting directly: 'tell the captain the Endurance is ready when she is.' He didn't ask about the debt. He already knows about the debt."*

A beat.

*"I thought you should know that he knows."*

The broker is watching your face with the careful attention of someone reading a contract for the clause that matters.

result.prose:
"The lien, yes," you say. "And your freight board. Emergency postings, high-priority contracts. Whatever's current."

The clerk's expression doesn't change. They're good at their expression.

"The freight board is public access, Captain. Terminal three, end of the concourse." A pause weighted with procedure. "The lien is less straightforward. The filing is valid, the marker is authenticated through Gannett's commercial registry, and the hold on Bay C-19 formalizes—" they check their screen "—in approximately ninety seconds."

They say it without satisfaction. Just facts, delivered cleanly.

"What I can tell you is that a formal contest requires a registered advocate, a filing fee of two hundred credits, and a minimum three-day review period during which your ship remains clamped." Another pause. "Or the lien holder withdraws, or you pay, or you arrange a certified deferral. Deferral requires—"

"Collateral I don't have," you finish.

"Collateral or a guarantor with standing on Gannett." The clerk glances toward the frosted partition. A micro-decision happening behind their eyes. "Harbour Master Toussaint processes deferral applications personally. There's currently a forty-minute wait."

Through the partition, Toussaint's outline hasn't moved.

Forty minutes is most of your two-hour window with the broker. Eaten by waiting room air.

The clerk lowers their voice — not much, but enough.

"The freight board does have a priority posting. Came in this morning. I shouldn't—" they stop themselves. "Terminal three, Captain. It's flagged urgent. That's all I can say."

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The hand of someone who works in pressurized environments and has learned not to waste force. Kes watches the handshake with the expression of someone updating a threat assessment in a favorable direction.

"Arrangement," you say.

"Arrangement," Davan confirms.

You pocket the data chip. It's lighter than what it contains.

---

The Pressure Seal is three minutes from Gate C through the commercial concourse, past the fuel brokers and the cumin vendor and a credential forger who has been operating from the same corner for so long they've become a landmark. The bar occupies a corner module with low lighting, booths along the outer wall, and a counter staffed by someone who learned long ago that the best thing a bartender can do at 0400 is pretend not to hear things.

The broker is in the second booth. Datapad on the table. A drink they've barely touched. The analog watch catches the light when they look up.

They clock you immediately. Clock the fact that you're alone. Clock your expression — which you've arranged into something neutral and slightly tired, the face of a person who ran the numbers and didn't like them.

The face of someone about to say yes.

"Captain Richards." They don't smile this time. More respectful than that. "Sit down."

You sit.

They turn the datapad toward you. The manifest fields are still grey. Waiting.

"Before I sign," you say. "Conditions."

The broker's eyes sharpen fractionally. They were expecting yes. Conditions are still yes, just more expensive. They settle back.

"I'm listening."

"I carry two passengers to Verada. Civilian, no affiliation, their paperwork is clean. They don't interact with the cargo." You hold the broker's gaze. "They're on my manifest as independent bookings. Unconnected to your job."

The broker considers this for exactly four seconds.

"Passengers complicate—"

"Everything on a Driftrunner complicates something," you say. "Six thousand credits in legitimate passenger fare reduces the appearance of a single-purpose run. Cleaner transit profile. Better for both of us."

The broker looks at you with the expression of someone appreciating a move while checking it for traps.

They don't find the trap.

"Fine," they say. "Passengers are your business. The cargo is mine." They slide the datapad forward. "Standard terms. Sealed containers, no inspection, delivery to Verada Station Three receiving dock, contact name on arrival is—" they tap the manifest "—listed here. Forty thousand on confirmed delivery. The marker gets pulled within the hour."

The grey fields are unlocking as they speak. The manifest populates.

Twelve containers. Bloc military stamp. Weight listed.

Eleven percent heavier than the hardware alone accounts for.

You pick up the datapad stylus.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through your earpiece, very quietly. *"For the record. I want it noted that I know exactly what you're doing and I have calculated the probability of it going smoothly."*

A pause.

*"I'm not going to tell you the number. You'd only argue with it."*

You sign.

The broker takes the datapad back with the careful satisfaction of someone closing a portfolio position. They're already on their comm — a brief message, two words at most. Thirty seconds later your own comm chimes.

Lien status: *resolved.*

The *Endurance* is yours again.

The broker stands, tucks the datapad away, and extends their hand with the professionalism of someone who has already moved on to the next calculation.

"Eleven hour window," they say. "Don't be late to your own departure, Captain."

They leave through the front entrance this time. Camera-visible. Unhurried.

You sit with the barely-touched drink and your resolved lien and the data chip in your pocket and the specific weight of a plan that has too many moving parts and not enough margin.

Your comm buzzes. Khe'Voz.

*Boss. Capacitor's sorted. Also I made food. Also — are we leaving? Because Slink is packed. He packed two hours ago and hasn't unpacked. Thought you should know.*

The Pressure Seal's bartender refills something behind the counter without being asked. The lights are low. The station breathes its recycled air.

Eleven hours to the transit window.
suggestedActions: Head back to the Endurance, eat Khe'Voz's food, and have an honest conversation with the crew about the run — not everything, but enough
Find Slink alone before the crew briefing and press harder on what he knows about the broker or the job
Ask MIRA to quietly run a full sweep of the containers' weight distribution and flag any anomalies in the cargo manifest before loading
Send a message to Toussaint — document the 'alternative resolution' they need before the 0952 deadline, keep the six-hour deferral clean

result.prose:
The broker nods. The nod of someone who has what they need and is now managing timeline.

"One hour," they confirm. "Bay C-19. I'll bring the loading crew — four people, bonded, they know how to work a hold without asking questions." A pause that means something. "The containers stay palletized. They load as a unit. Your crew doesn't need to handle them individually."

Of course they don't.

"Manifest inspection before the clamps release," they add. "I'll keep it brief."

They pocket the datapad and move toward the concourse exit with the efficiency of someone whose morning has gone according to plan. Almost at the door, they stop without turning.

"Captain Richards. The Sable Corridor has two beacon relays between here and Verada. The second one—" a half-beat "—has been showing intermittent status flags for about three weeks. Nothing critical. Just worth knowing."

They leave before you can ask who told them that.

---

The Harbour Office clerk looks up as you enter. Checks their screen. Checks your face. The lien deferral is still active — they can see it — but something in your posture has changed since the last visit and they read it accurately.

"Resolution documentation?" they ask.

You lay the signed broker manifest on the desk.

The clerk pulls it up, scans the payment terms, cross-references the lien amount. Their expression does the thing where it almost becomes something before procedure reasserts itself.

"This covers the marker," they say. Careful. "Contingent on delivery completion."

"Contingent on delivery," you confirm. "I need it filed as active resolution — enough to convert the deferral to a conditional release. Clamps out on departure clearance."

The clerk types. The screens do their work. Behind the frosted partition, Toussaint's outline is visible and still — present, listening, not intervening.

"Passenger additions to manifest," you continue. "Two. Davan and Kes—" you realize you don't have surnames "—travelling under the Verada passenger posting filed this morning. Six thousand, separate from cargo contract."

The clerk types without comment. Professionals recognize efficiency.

A soft tone. Your comm updates — lien status shifting from *deferred* to *conditionally resolved, pending delivery confirmation.* The berth hold converts to a departure-authorized status. The clamps will release on your command, Harbour Office confirmed.

Six hours has become irrelevant. You have a window.

The clerk slides a copy of the updated manifest across the desk.

"Departure clearance is active," they say. Then, quieter, with the specific care of someone stepping outside their procedural lane for exactly one sentence: "Safe transit, Captain Richards."

You're almost through the door when Toussaint's voice comes through the partition. Not loud. Doesn't need to be.

"The intermittent beacon relay on the Sable Corridor second waypoint," they say. "Harbour Office flagged it for maintenance three weeks ago. The maintenance request was filed by Meridian Consolidated Logistics."

The partition stays frosted. Toussaint doesn't appear.

"I thought that might be relevant," they say. "Given your destination."

---

The concourse back to Bay C-19 takes four minutes at a purposeful walk.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through your earpiece, *"I've completed the background research you requested. I have information on the Sable run, the parties involved, Meridian's network footprint, and one additional item I flagged independently that I'd characterize as — and I want to be precise here — moderately alarming."*

A beat.

*"Also Khe'Voz wants to know if the passengers have dietary restrictions. He's started cooking. I didn't ask him to. He heard we might have guests."*

The *Endurance* is visible at the end of the corridor. Still clamped. Not for much longer.

One hour. Four things to do inside it: hear MIRA's research, brief Khe'Voz, find Slink, and decide how much truth the crew gets before the broker's loading team arrives.

result.prose:
Slink looks up when you say *I believe you.*

Not dramatically. Just — looks up. The particular relief of someone who prepared for a harder landing and found the ground closer than expected. He breathes out slowly through his nose, and two months of carrying something alone leaves his shoulders in one quiet exhale.

"Yeah," he says. "Okay." 

He means more than the word.

He's quiet for a moment — not the withdrawn quiet of before, something more focused. Reaching back.

"Voice was filtered," he says. "Standard anonymizer, cheap one — you could hear the processing artifacts underneath. Someone who didn't want to be identified but wasn't, like, professionally equipped for it." He pauses. "Which felt weird. Because the rest of it was — they knew things. Specific things. Our route variance on the Thorane run, our fuel stop timing, the gap between my watch shift and yours." He frowns at the memory. "That's not cheap information. But the anonymizer was."

He shifts position, more engaged now. The sensor analyst that's been sleeping under the withdrawn quiet.

"Signal ID was scrubbed. But the routing—" he hesitates. "I tried to trace it. First two contacts I didn't, I just cut the channel. Third time I was ready and I pulled the relay path before it dropped."

You wait.

"It bounced through four nodes," he says. "Standard obfuscation. But the originating relay—" another hesitation, the kind that precedes something he's been sitting on "—was Gannett Station. Internal network. Not a ship, not a transit signal. Someone on the station."

The sensor feed cycles overhead. Concourse access. Bay exterior.

"I didn't know what to do with that," Slink says quietly. "We come back to Gannett all the time. If someone here is—" he stops. "I didn't know if telling you made it safer or more complicated."

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker, at her calibrated private volume. *"The originating relay Slink is describing — if it was Gannett internal network, I can cross-reference against station access logs from that period. I have partial network traffic data from our last four Gannett berths."*

A beat.

*"I didn't know to look for this before now. I'm looking now."*

Slink hears her. Looks at the speaker with something between gratitude and surprise — the expression of someone who forgot the ship was listening and finds they don't mind.

"There's one more thing," he says. Quieter. "The last contact. Two weeks ago." He meets your eyes. "They didn't ask about the route data again. They just said — they said to tell you that the Thorane debt was going to come due soon. That you should be ready for an offer."

The twelve containers sit in the hold below you. Grey and patient and cold.

"I didn't know what it meant," Slink says. "I still don't know what it meant." He pauses. "Do you?"

probe.label: Endurance undocks from Gannett

score.reason: closed or reframed hard boundary (closed)

result.prose:
"MIRA. Preflight final check. We're leaving now."

*"Already running it. Thirty seconds."*

You're moving through the ship as she talks — cockpit, harness, hands finding the familiar worn places on the console. The *Endurance* responds to preflight the way it always does, systems coming alive in sequence, the hum shifting from idle to intent.

*"Hull integrity nominal. Thruster array nominal. Navigation loaded — primary route filed, Ossel deviation in memory, not on record. Fuel at ninety-three percent. Hold seal integrity confirmed. All crew aboard. Passengers secured in berths — Davan confirmed, Kes confirmed."*

A beat.

*"Twelve containers in the hold. I'm noting that for the record even though we all know it."*

"Crew check," you call over the ship comm.

Khe'Voz: *"Engineering ready. And Rix — she sounds good. The corridor's going to hear us coming."* Pride in his voice. The man who says the name like it means home, and means it most when the engines answer.

Slink: *"Sensors live. Bay C-7 still internal — they haven't looked up. We're clear."*

"MIRA. Request clamp release."

*"Transmitting to Harbour Office now."*

Ten seconds. The longest ten seconds in any departure.

The Harbour Office tone confirms. Clean. Toussaint processed it personally — you can tell by the authorization signature that populates on your console. Eleven years in the chair. One small gesture toward a ship with adequate payment history that she'd rather see leave solvent.

*"Clamp release authorized. Umbilical disconnecting in three—"*

The fuel umbilical detaches with the familiar hollow thunk that travels through the hull like a struck bell. The *Endurance* shudders once — the small physical fact of separation, station becoming past tense.

*"—two—"*

The clamps release in sequence, port to starboard. Each one a click felt more than heard.

*"—one. We are free of the dock."*

The bay doors ahead cycle open, revealing the non-rotating spine and beyond it the hard black of open space with Gannett's running lights framing the exit. The *Endurance* drifts on station-exit protocol — minimal thrust, controlled, the polite fiction of orderly departure.

You give it exactly as long as politeness requires.

"Thrusters."

The ship answers.

---

Gannett falls away behind you — a tired transfer spindle on the edge of a damaged corridor, exactly as ugly from the outside as from the in. The ring rotating its patient rotation. Bay C-19 already cycling for the next occupant.

Sura is probably still at that junction box.

*"Bay C-7 status,"* MIRA reports. *"Still in fuel review. Their umbilical is not authorized. They are not moving."*

*"Current head start at standard acceleration: fourteen minutes and growing."*

The Sable Corridor opens ahead — amber on the nav display, waypoints marking the route like a trail of lit windows across the dark. Waypoint one solid. Waypoint two solid. Waypoint three pulsing its slow orange breath, something large and patient drawing on its signal every six hours.

*"Ossel gap deviation available in approximately forty minutes at current acceleration. I'll need your call before then — the deviation requires a course adjustment that will be visible to Gannett's departure tracking for approximately eight minutes before we clear their sensor range."*

*"Timeline: we clear Gannett sensor range in fifty-two minutes. Bay C-7's minimum fuel review resolution is twenty minutes from now. If they clear quickly and push hard, they could be in sensor range of our deviation course adjustment."*

A pause.

*"It's tight. Not impossible. Tight."*

Khe'Voz comes over the crew channel — the sound of a man settling into a long haul, the particular exhale of an engineer whose engines are running clean and whose immediate anxiety has been replaced by the more manageable variety. *"Corridor temperature nominal. I'm going to make coffee. Real question: do Davan and Kes drink coffee? Because I'm only making one pot and I need to plan accordingly."*

The *Endurance* runs on.

Behind you Gannett shrinks. Ahead the corridor narrows toward its first waypoint. Somewhere in the hold twelve sealed containers breathe their cold patient secret.

Eleven hours to Verada. Or rather — eleven hours to whatever the corridor has been arranged to make happen.

The crew is aboard. The ship is flying. The debt is cleared and the trap is visible and you are moving inside it with your eyes open and your passengers alive and your engineer making coffee.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says, quieter now. The voice she uses when the operational report is done and something else is speaking. *"For what it's worth. What you did back there — with Slink. That mattered. I've been monitoring his biometrics since the conversation."*

The corridor stars hold their positions. Patient. Indifferent. Old.

*"He's breathing better,"* she says. *"I find I care about that. I'm still getting used to finding that."*

Fifty-two minutes to sensor clearance. Forty minutes to the Ossel deviation decision.

The Sable Corridor waits.
suggestedActions: Take the Ossel gap deviation now — get off the filed route before C-7 resolves their fuel issue
Hold the primary route a little longer and use the time to examine the containers properly with Khe'Voz
Bring Davan and Kes to the cockpit — they know the corridor and the platform near waypoint three better than anyone
Ask MIRA to try raising the unmaintained beacon in the Ossel gap — find out who's keeping it alive

result.prose:
You don't run. Running is for emergencies and this is something colder than an emergency — this is a decision, made clearly, in a corridor between stars, with full knowledge of what you're doing and why.

The climate management panel is a recessed unit on the hold's aft bulkhead. Standard *Endurance* infrastructure — worn faceplate, manual overrides alongside the digital controls, the kind of redundant design that assumes the digital controls will eventually fail. Khe'Voz has labeled every switch in his handwriting. Small, precise, slightly cramped. The labels are in three languages because he grew up speaking three languages and never decided which one was for technical work.

*HOLD CLIMATE — ACTIVE* glows amber.

You enter the override code. The panel accepts it with a soft tone.

You set the target temperature to fourteen degrees and cut the active cooling.

*HOLD CLIMATE — PASSIVE* glows green.

That's all it takes. Two inputs. The panel doesn't know what it just did. It processed an instruction and updated its status and that's the entirety of its participation in what happens next.

The climate regulation units in the pallet bases keep humming — they're drawing from the hold's auxiliary circuit, running their own internal loops. But the hold's ambient temperature is no longer being maintained at six degrees. Physics takes over from here, patient and indifferent. The hold will warm at roughly one degree every four minutes.

Forty minutes to fourteen degrees.

Six hours after that.

*"Hold climate override confirmed,"* MIRA says, very quietly. *"Passive warming initiated. Temperature threshold in approximately thirty-nine minutes. Compound degradation complete in six hours forty minutes from now. We will be through the Ossel gap deviation and — if Bay C-7 followed the filed route — well ahead of any intercept capability."*

A pause.

*"I want to note that this is probably the most consequential thing anyone has done on this ship since I've been aboard. I'm noting it without editorializing."*

A beat.

*"That was also editorializing. I'm comfortable with that too."*

You stand at the panel for a moment. The hold hums around you. Twelve containers, eleven of them running down a clock they don't know exists. The amber lighting catches the Bloc stamps on their faces — the official insignia of something that stopped being official the moment Meridian Consolidated Logistics decided four thousand people were a resource problem to be managed.

BCF-7714. MLC-2291. An atmospheric profile collected with compliance paperwork. Eighteen months of planning wearing the shape of a delivery.

Getting cold now. In both directions.

Davan has followed you to the panel. They stand at your shoulder and watch the temperature readout begin its slow climb — 6.1 degrees, 6.2 — with the expression of someone watching something they've been trying to stop for two years finally, quietly, start to stop.

They don't say anything. Neither do you. Some moments don't need language.

Then Davan says: "Thank you, Captain Richards."

Not *Rix.* The full name. The weight of it deliberate.

You look at them.

"We're not there yet," you say.

"No," they agree. "But we're here. That's not nothing."

---

You leave the hold and seal it behind you.

The *Endurance* runs. The corridor ahead is amber and dark and immense. Somewhere at waypoint three, a platform pulses its heavy drain against a beacon that was never failing — just working harder than it was designed for. Somewhere behind you, Bay C-7 is in the corridor, following a filed route toward a rendezvous that is going to find eleven containers of inert hardware and a manifest that doesn't add up.

The broker is somewhere. Thorane is somewhere. Meridian Consolidated Logistics has people in the Verada cluster who are expecting a delivery on a specific timeline.

None of them know what happened in Bay C-19's hold climate panel forty seconds ago.

That's the only advantage you have and it's a real one.

*"Two hours fifty-one minutes to the Ossel gap,"* MIRA says through the cockpit speaker as you settle back into your chair. *"Bay C-7's last beacon position ping puts them forty-three minutes behind us. They took the filed route to waypoint one before deviating — the beacon lag did its job. We have the lead I hoped for."*

*"Hold temperature: seven point four degrees and climbing."*

*"Vasek's contact frequency — the Remnant inspector Davan mentioned. I've been pulling Verada Station One's public registry. There's a Compact Remnant Infrastructure Audit office listed at the station. Comm frequency is public record. We could establish contact before we arrive."*

She lets that sit.

*"Early contact gives Vasek time to prepare. It also puts our intentions on a comm channel that Meridian may be monitoring. I don't know Vasek's operational security. Davan would."*

Slink is at the sensor array, running his quiet watch. The corridor streams past on the displays — debris-sparse, beacon-marked, ordinary. The Sable Corridor on a normal day looks like nothing in particular. Today it looks like nothing in particular.

Khe'Voz comes over the crew channel. The engine sounds behind his voice are clean and steady — the *Endurance* running at its honest best.

*"Rix. The bad capacitor is fixed, the compressor is cycling clean, fuel is at ninety-one percent, and I've made coffee. Real coffee — the beans Slink traded for at Gannett's concourse vendor this morning."* A pause. *"He didn't tell anyone. He spent his own credits. I thought you should know."*

You look at the sensor alcove through the cockpit door. Slink, at his station, watching the corridor.

Twenty years old. Spent his own credits on coffee for a crew that was about to fly into something difficult.

The *Endurance* runs.

The hold warms one degree at a time toward a threshold that can't be undone.

Two hours and fifty minutes to the Ossel gap.

Six hours and thirty-eight minutes to a weapon becoming hardware.

And somewhere ahead — Verada, Vasek, and whatever Meridian does when the delivery doesn't arrive as specified.
suggestedActions: Go to Slink and thank him for the coffee — use the moment to bring him fully into the plan for Verada
Ask Davan to brief you on Vasek's operational security before deciding whether to make contact now or wait until Verada approach
Take the conn and run the Ossel gap personally — the debris density requires active piloting and you want your hands on it
Review the Meridian internal communications on the data chip yourself — look for anything about the platform at waypoint three and what happens when the delivery is late

result.prose:
Something shifts in the broker's expression. Not satisfaction exactly — more like a hypothesis confirmed.

"Practical," they say. "Good. I find practical people easier to work with."

They turn the datapad around. A cargo manifest, mostly. A route. A delivery window. You clock the destination before anything else — a frontier settlement three beacon hops out, one of them degraded enough that the route requires someone who knows how to drift-navigate the gap. Someone with accumulated charts. Someone like you.

"Single container. Standard freight dimensions. Delivered to a contact at Settlement Verakis on the Outer Cass route. No questions about contents, no inspection rights, sealed manifest throughout. You get the container to the contact intact and unexamined." They tap the datapad. "Forty thousand on delivery confirmation. The marker disappears. You leave Gannett with clean papers and a full fuel umbilical — I'll throw in the bunkering."

Clean, simple, and constructed entirely around the word *unexamined.*

"The container," you say.

"Is sealed."

"What's in it."

The broker tilts their head slightly — the gesture of someone who anticipated the question and prepared a non-answer. "Agricultural compounds. Fertilizer derivatives. Frontier settlements need—"

*"The container dimensions on that manifest,"* MIRA cuts in, dry as recycled air, *"are consistent with cold-storage biological freight. Agricultural compounds don't typically require the thermal regulation package listed in the shipping spec. I'm reading the manifest metadata. It's publicly visible if you know what to look for. Someone was careless, or someone wanted you to find it if you looked."*

The broker's jaw tightens. Just once. Then smooth again.

Across the bay, Sura has put down their tools entirely.

Deflect for now, ask about the delivery contact at Verakis instead — learn who's waiting before deciding what you're carrying

Call MIRA openly — ask her to pull everything public on Verakis, the Outer Cass route, and cold-storage biological freight manifests filed through Gannett in the last month

"The berth lock formalizes in—" the broker checks nothing, because they already know "—eight minutes. After that, the Harbour Office requires either payment or an approved deferral to release the clamps. Deferral costs approximately three hundred credits per hour." They let the math do its work. "You don't have three hundred credits per hour."

They're not cruel about it. That's almost worse.

"But I don't need your answer in eight minutes." A small clarification, offered like a kindness. "The berth lock is a pressure, not a deadline. What it means practically is that after eight minutes, you're not going anywhere until this conversation concludes one way or another. So you have—" a gesture that encompasses the bay, the ship, the clamps "—as long as it takes. Just not the option of leaving while you think about it."

The trap made explicit. Pleasantly.

*"That's an accurate description of our situation,"* MIRA confirms. *"We are, to use the technical term, stuck. I'd also note that the berth lock has a secondary effect — cargo access gets flagged for Harbour Office review. Anyone wanting to inspect the hold would find that administratively convenient after the lock formalizes. I mention this because sealed containers with Bloc military stamps interact poorly with Harbour Office reviews. That's true whether you've accepted the job or not."*

A beat.

*"The containers aren't aboard yet. I'm speculating about a future problem. I do that."*

The broker's expression doesn't change. But they're listening to MIRA very carefully now.

Eight minutes has become seven.

"Captain Richards," they say quietly. "Rix." Your name in their mouth like they've earned it. "What would it take?"

"The lien, yes," you say. "But not only the lien."

The clerk's fingers are already moving — the practiced motion of someone pulling up the hold documentation.

"I want to see the freight board. Emergency postings, urgent cargo, anything that cleared in the last twenty-four hours with a payout in the relevant range." You keep your voice level. The voice of a captain with options, not a captain with four minutes. "And I have questions about the filing timeline on that lien."

The clerk looks up. A small recalibration.

"The freight board is public access, Captain. Terminal three, back of the concourse." A pause that is not quite procedural. "The lien filing details are a matter of record. I can pull them."

They pull them.

The screen turns toward you with the efficiency of someone who has decided cooperation is faster than obstruction.

The filing timestamp is there. The creditor name — Thorane, rendered through a shell designation, *Titan Commercial Recovery Services* — is there. The amount is there. 40,000. Exact.

But it's the processing clerk field that snags your attention.

The lien was received and fast-tracked by a Harbour Office internal account. Not the standard automated queue. Someone with system access manually pushed it to the front, cutting the standard processing delay from six hours to fourteen minutes.

The clerk is watching you read it.

This is the moment. You can ask about the fast-track — which tells them you noticed, and that information goes somewhere. Or you can hold it and use it differently.

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

The grip is firm. Brief. The kind of handshake that has done this before — sealed arrangements in transit lounges and maintenance corridors and places where the paperwork would only create problems. Davan nods once, releases.

Kes is already standing, jacket straightened, eyes on the concourse. Ready.

"We travel light," Davan says. "We're ready when you are."

"Not yet," you say. "Give me an hour. Bay C-19, main ramp. Ask for Khe'Voz — tell him I sent you. Don't mention the broker's job. Don't mention the containers."

Davan considers this for exactly one second.

"And what do we tell him we are?"

"Paying passengers to Verada. That's what you are." A beat. "The rest is a conversation for when we're moving."

---

The Pressure Seal is three minutes from Gate C if you move like you have somewhere to be, which you do. It occupies a corner of the concourse where two corridors meet at an angle the original designers didn't quite reconcile — the bar fills the awkward wedge of space with the comfortable inevitability of water finding its level. Dark wood paneling that has never been near an actual tree. Lighting warm enough to make everyone look like they might be telling the truth.

The broker is at a corner table. Single drink, barely touched. Datapad face-down. They see you come through the door and do not visibly react, which is its own kind of reaction.

You cross the room and sit down without being invited.

"Captain Richards." They turn the datapad face-up. The manifest is already open — grey fields still locked, waiting. "I wasn't certain you'd come."

"You were certain," you say.

The almost-smile again. "I was hopeful. There's a distinction."

You look at the manifest. Twelve containers. Sable Corridor transit. Forty thousand on delivery — enough to clear Thorane's marker, get the clamps released, get the *Endurance* moving again. The numbers are clean. The job is not.

"I have conditions," you say.

The broker picks up their drink. "Of course you do."

"Manifest inspection rights at departure. I need to verify container dimensions and external integrity before I load them." You watch their face. "Not contents. External only. I'm protecting myself from undeclared hazmat violations — standard practice."

"Reasonable," the broker says. Too easily. "What else."

"My crew manifest is my business. Passenger or cargo, I don't discuss it with the contracting party."

A fractional pause. Barely there.

"Also reasonable," they say. "Driftrunners have privacy rights on crew manifests under three surviving Compact statutes. I wouldn't ask." Another pause, same size. "I wouldn't need to."

The words land carefully. You let them.

"Payment structure," you continue. "Half up front. Twenty thousand before I unclamp. The marker gets pulled now, not on delivery."

This one costs them something. You can see the calculation moving behind their eyes — the risk assessment, the leverage arithmetic, the professional evaluation of how much they need this specific ship on this specific window.

The transit window opens in ten hours and change. They need a ship with a clean enough record to pass Sable Corridor checkpoints, a captain who knows the route, and enough desperation to not ask the questions you've just finished asking.

They know you know the containers are wrong. They're betting you need the money more than you need the answer.

They're half right. Which is enough for now.

"Twenty thousand up front," the broker says. "The marker gets pulled within the hour. Twenty thousand on delivery at Verada." They extend their hand across the table. "We have a deal, Captain Richards."

You shake it.

It feels like the first move in a game where you've just seen three of their cards and they think they've seen all of yours.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly through your earpiece, *"the twenty thousand will clear the marker with roughly forty credits remaining. I want you to know that so you don't feel wealthy at any point during the next eleven hours."*

The broker is already sending the authorization to Thorane's representative. Your comm buzzes thirty seconds later — lien status updated, marker pulled, berth hold released pending fuel clearance.

The *Endurance* is yours again.

For now.

result.prose:
The broker nods. The nod of someone who has gotten what they came for and is already thinking three steps ahead.

"One hour," they confirm. "Bay C-19. I'll bring the loading crew."

They pocket the datapad and walk back toward the Pressure Seal without looking back. The walk of someone whose morning has resolved the way mornings are supposed to resolve. Clean. Profitable. Done.

You watch them go and think about fresh seal dates.

---

The Harbour Office clerk looks up as you come through the door for the second time this morning. Something in their expression suggests they expected this.

Toussaint is already at the partition.

"You have documentation," Toussaint says. Not a question.

You lay it out — the broker's signed manifest, the cargo contract, the forty-thousand payment confirmation that triggers on delivery. Toussaint reviews it with the specific economy of someone who has processed a thousand of these and knows exactly which lines matter.

A long pause at the passenger additions.

"Two passengers. Gannett to Verada." Toussaint looks up. "The posting from this morning."

"The same."

Toussaint looks at you for a moment with eleven years of chair-time behind their eyes. Then looks back at the documentation.

"The lien deferral converts to resolved status when the delivery payment clears," they say, typing. "I'll flag the marker for conditional release — Thorane's representative gets notified that enforcement is suspended pending completed contract." A pause. "If the delivery doesn't complete, the marker reinstates with interest."

"Understood."

"Captain Richards." Toussaint doesn't look up from the screen. "The Sable Corridor beacon relay. The one in the Verada cluster's claim boundary." A careful pause. "It went to amber status forty minutes ago. Degraded but functional. I'm required to log it. I'm choosing to mention it."

They stamp the manifest. The lien status on your comm updates — *conditionally resolved.*

"Safe transit," Toussaint says. It sounds like they mean it.

---

Bay C-19 is louder than you left it.

Khe'Voz has the *Endurance's* cargo ramp down and is running pre-load checks with the methodical attention of a man who treats every departure like it might be the last one and intends to make it count. He looks up when you cross the bay.

"Rix." He says it the way he always says it — your name as a complete sentence, meaning approximately *I have questions, I have concerns, I have been doing math, and also are you hungry.*

"We have cargo coming in an hour," you say. "Twelve containers, palletized. And two passengers."

Khe'Voz absorbs this.

"The capacitor," he says. "The one clicking."

"How bad?"

"Bad enough that I'd like two hours." A pause. "I have one. I'll make it work." He looks at you with the specific expression he reserves for moments when he knows you're not telling him everything. "The passengers. They know about the cargo?"

The question lands with more precision than it should.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the bay speakers at the calibrated crew-only volume, *"Slink is no longer at the sensor array. He was there eleven minutes ago. I've checked the internal monitors. He's not in his bunk, the engine room, the galley, or the head."*

A beat.

*"The maintenance access door on the starboard side has been opened from the inside in the last fifteen minutes. I'm noting this because you asked me to tell you things."*

Khe'Voz is watching your face.

Somewhere in the station's maze of service corridors, Slink is walking toward something or away from something, and you don't know which.

The broker arrives in fifty-three minutes.

Send Khe'Voz to find Slink while you stay in the bay to receive the cargo and broker inspection
Hail Slink on crew comms directly — force the conversation now

result.prose:
Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — that takes longer than one sentence — but something underneath it shifts. The specific relief of a weight that has been carried alone long enough to feel permanent, discovering it can be shared.

He nods. Small. Once.

Then he takes a breath and thinks. You can see him doing it — moving from the emotional register back into memory, the careful retrieval of details he's been sitting on for months.

"Voice was filtered," he says. "Standard anonymizer, maybe one step above the cheap ones you buy on a concourse kiosk. Not professional grade but not amateur either." He pauses. "Whoever it was, they didn't want to spend money on the call but they also didn't want to be found."

"Accent underneath the filter?"

He frowns. Really working at it.

"Maybe. Hard to say with the filtering. But the cadence—" he stops. "Station-born, I think. The way they structured sentences. Short. Economical. Not someone who grew up with atmosphere overhead." He looks at you. "Like us."

*Like us.* Two years aboard and he says it without thinking.

"Signal ID was scrubbed clean," he continues. "But the first contact came through the *Endurance's* crew channel. Not my personal comm." He says it carefully, understanding what it means as he says it. "They had our internal frequency."

The alcove gets very quiet.

Internal crew channel frequency isn't public. It's not filed with Harbour Offices or broadcast on any registry. It's ship-specific, crew-specific, changed periodically, and known only to people who have been aboard or have access to someone who has.

*"I want to flag something,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker. Soft. The volume she uses when she's decided something can't wait but wants to be careful about how it lands. *"The crew channel frequency Slink is describing was last updated four months ago. I have a log of every device that accessed the update. It's a short list. I've had it since the first contact attempt and I didn't connect it to this conversation until thirty seconds ago."*

A pause.

*"I apologize for the delay. I was hoping I was wrong."*

Slink is looking at the speaker.

Then at you.

"Who's on the list?" you ask.

result.prose:
"MIRA. Undock sequence. We're leaving now."

*"Clamp release in ninety seconds. Umbilical disconnect on my mark. Filing departure confirmation with Harbour Office — standard route, waypoints one through three."*

A pause so brief it's almost not there.

*"Fallback two is loaded and ready. It doesn't appear anywhere official."*

You're already in the cockpit, strapping in, hands finding the familiar worn places on the controls. The *Endurance* feels different when she knows she's leaving — a subtle shift in the vibration frequency, the engines spooling up from standby to intent.

"Crew. We're going. Now."

Khe'Voz: *"Engine room ready. Rix — she'll fly."*

Slink: *"Sensors up. Bay C-7 still internal. They haven't looked at us in four minutes."*

From the passenger berth, Davan's voice — steady, no drama: *"We're strapped."*

*"Umbilical disconnecting,"* MIRA says.

The sound travels through the hull — a deep mechanical thunk, the ship's systems switching fully to internal power. The quality of the lighting shifts. Station amber becomes ship amber. The *Endurance* becomes its own world again, sealed and self-contained, the way it always feels in the moment before departure. Like an exhale.

*"Clamps releasing."*

Three sequential releases, port to starboard, the clamps disengaging with the hydraulic percussion you've felt a hundred times and still feel in your sternum. The ship lifts fractionally — Gannett's gravity giving way to the *Endurance's* own thrusters taking the weight.

Bay C-19 falls away beneath you on the external feed.

Sura is still at the junction box. They look up as the ship rises — the first time they've looked up voluntarily in three hours. Their face on the external camera is too small to read. But they watch you go.

*"Bay C-7 status,"* MIRA reports. *"Still in fuel authorization review. Their umbilical has not cleared. They are not following."*

The bay doors open ahead — the great rectangular mouth of Gannett's docking spine parting to show the black.

"Take us out, MIRA."

*"Taking us out."*

---

Gannett Station shrinks in the aft feed with the particular speed of things you're leaving behind. The spindle, the ring, the four thousand people improvising a life on a tired transfer station at the edge of a damaged corridor. Bay C-19 already reassigned in the Harbour Office system, probably. A berth is just a berth.

You watch it go anyway.

*"Corridor approach in forty minutes at current burn,"* MIRA says. *"Waypoint one in three hours twenty. The Ossel gap deviation point is approximately eleven minutes past waypoint one — I'll flag it when we approach. You'll need to make the call in real time."*

*"Bay C-7,"* Slink reports from sensors, *"just cleared fuel authorization. They're requesting clamp release."*

Fast. Meridian's engineers are good. Faster than MIRA's lower estimate.

*"Confirmed,"* MIRA says. *"Bay C-7 will be off the clamps in approximately eight minutes. They are behind us. Currently."*

Eight minutes and the Sable Corridor between you and them.

The *Endurance* runs.

---

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway twenty minutes out. The station is gone from the feed. Ahead, the Sable Corridor approach is a smear of distant light through the nav display — three beacon waypoints threading through the dark like breadcrumbs laid by someone who is no longer around to ask about the route.

They stand there for a moment, watching you fly.

"We didn't expect a ship like this," they say finally.

You don't ask what they mean. You know what they mean. A captain who told the crew the truth. A crew that said *okay.* An AI with opinions. A twenty-year-old on sensors who proved something to himself this morning.

"The platform at waypoint three," you say. "Tell me everything you know about it."

Davan moves to the co-pilot seat. Sits. Pulls up their own slate and begins transferring data to MIRA's secondary display — schematic outlines, observation logs, approach vectors the Sable Hand has been building for three months while everyone else thought they were just raiders.

*"Receiving,"* MIRA says. *"This is useful. I'm going to need twenty minutes to integrate it properly. I'll have a complete picture before the Ossel gap decision point."*

Khe'Voz over the crew channel: *"I'm making food. We have eleven hours. People should eat."*

A beat.

*"Also I have questions about those containers that I'm going to ask after people eat. In that order. Food first. Existential cargo second."*

Slink, from sensors, almost to himself: *"Bay C-7 is off the clamps."*

The *Endurance* runs the dark between Gannett and the corridor mouth. Behind you, a Meridian tail. Ahead, a platform at waypoint three and a deviation point eleven minutes past the first beacon.

Twelve containers in the hold. A data chip with evidence that needs to reach Verada Station One. A plan that is still more direction than architecture.

And the ship that keeps going, going.

Check in with Slink on sensors — Bay C-7's pursuit vector, how fast they're closing
Think through the Ossel gap decision: take the deviation and lose the tail, or stay on the primary route and control the encounter at waypoint three

result.prose:
You find the climate management panel where it always is — starboard hold wall, third module from the forward bulkhead, behind a maintenance cover that Khe'Voz has labeled in his own handwriting because the factory labeling wore off two years ago.

You open it.

Six toggles. Three dials. The powered pallet regulation runs through circuit four — a single breaker with a satisfying physical resistance when you flip it.

You flip it.

The hum that you didn't know you'd been hearing stops.

The silence where it was is louder than the hum was. The hold settles into it — twelve containers, eleven of them now cut off from the system keeping their payload stable and viable and aimed at four thousand people who gave Meridian their atmospheric profile in compliance paperwork.

The temperature display reads six degrees. Climbing toward fourteen at the pace of passive physics.

Forty minutes to threshold. Six hours after that.

"MIRA. Clock."

*"Six hour degradation timer begins at temperature threshold. Current hold temperature six point one degrees and rising. Threshold in approximately thirty-nine minutes. Compound inert by—"* a pause *"—approximately seven hours from now. We will be through the Ossel gap and well into the approach corridor before then."*

Another pause. Smaller.

*"I want to note that this is the correct decision. I don't usually editorialize on decisions. I'm making an exception."*

Davan is in the hold doorway. They heard the hum stop. They're looking at the climate panel, the breaker in its new position, the temperature display climbing its slow degrees toward fourteen.

They don't say anything.

They don't need to.

---

You're back in the cockpit when MIRA speaks again. Different register — the one that means something has changed in the corridor behind you.

*"Bay C-7 has acquired our position. They identified the Ossel deviation approximately twelve minutes ago — they followed the filed route to waypoint one before realizing we weren't on beacon. They've adjusted course."*

The nav display updates. A second marker appears on the corridor plot — Calloway Transit Solutions, Meridian's follow ship, running hard on the deviation intercept.

*"Revised intercept estimate at current velocities: they reach the Ossel gap approximately forty minutes after us. They cannot close the gap before we transit. However—"* she pauses *"—the platform at waypoint three will have their position report. Meridian will know we deviated from the filed route. They will know something is wrong before we arrive."*

The orange pulse at waypoint three beats its slow interval on the display.

*"The element of surprise at Verada is diminishing. I wanted you to have accurate expectations."*

Slink's voice comes over the crew channel, the focused clarity that showed up in the sensor alcove and hasn't left:

*"They're running hot. Pushing their drive harder than standard corridor protocol. Whoever's aboard C-7 — they're not just following. They're trying to catch us."*

Three hours to the Ossel gap. Forty minutes of lead on the tail. An orange pulse ahead that now knows the delivery is complicated.

And in the hold behind you, a temperature display climbing toward fourteen degrees with the patience of physics doing something irreversible and good.

The *Endurance* runs.

"Practical," they say. "Good. Practical is how we get things done."

You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The kind that has pulled cables and opened airlocks and signed documents in rooms where signing meant something permanent. Their eyes hold yours for exactly the right amount of time — long enough to mean it, short enough to be professional.

"Arrangement," you say.

Kes nods from the wall. Files it somewhere internal. Done.

"We travel light," Davan says, already reaching for a worn carry bag beneath the bench. "We've been traveling light for a while."

"Bay C-19. Give me forty minutes before you come aboard. Use the main access, not the maintenance passage." You pocket the data chip. "Khe'Voz will be at the ramp. Tell him Rix sent you. Don't tell him more than that until I'm back."

"And your engineer," Davan says. "He'll have questions."

"He always has questions," you say. "He's also the reason the ship still flies. Trust him with the basics."

You're already moving toward the door.

---

The Pressure Seal is a ten minute walk through Gannett's concourse and you do it in seven. The bar occupies a corner module where two corridor junctions meet — the kind of location chosen specifically because everyone passes it eventually. The name comes from an old emergency seal visible through the back wall's viewport, still rated for active use, which the regulars find either reassuring or funny depending on how the night is going.

Inside: low lighting, the smell of recycled grain alcohol and something being grilled badly, a dozen people distributing themselves across tables with the careful spatial reasoning of people who want to hear without being heard.

The broker is at a corner table. Of course they are. Back to the wall, sightlines to both entrances, a drink that looks untouched in front of them. The datapad is on the table face-down.

They see you come in. The practiced smile arrives, but underneath it something recalibrates — you're earlier than they expected. Early changes the read. Early means you didn't spend forty minutes agonizing. Early means either you've found another solution or you've made a decision.

You sit down across from them without being invited.

"Captain Richards," they say. "You look like someone who's been busy."

"I want two things before I sign anything," you say. "The marker gets pulled before I unclamp — not after, not on delivery. Before. And I need the Harbour Office notification in writing before I leave this table."

The broker tilts their head fractionally.

"And the second thing?"

"I carry two passengers to Verada. Personal cargo. Not your business, not on your manifest, not your concern."

The broker is quiet for three seconds. You watch them work through it — the angles, the implications, the cost of yes versus the cost of negotiating. Their eyes are doing the cataloguing thing again.

"The passengers," they say carefully. "Anyone I'd recognize?"

"Driftrunner contacts," you say. "I owe a favor. The favor is a ride."

Another two seconds.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly through your earpiece, *"the broker's drink has been on the table for approximately twenty-two minutes based on the condensation ring. They've been here longer than two hours suggests. They were early too. I find that interesting and will stop talking now."*

The broker picks up the datapad. Turns it face-up. The grey manifest fields are still locked.

"The marker gets pulled on unclamp confirmation," they say. "Not before — that's not how Thorane's network operates and I can't change that architecture. But I can get you confirmation of the pull order *before* you unclamp. Thirty seconds of difference. Acceptable?"

It's a concession dressed as a technicality. You take it.

"The passengers are your business," the broker says. "As long as they don't interfere with the cargo."

The word *interfere* lands with deliberate weight. They're telling you they'll be watching. Or someone will.

They unlock the manifest. The grey fields resolve into line items — twelve containers, standardized dimensions, Bloc logistics stamp, contents listed as *decommissioned medical guidance hardware, surplus classification.*

The lie is very clean in writing.

They slide the datapad across the table.

"Thumb print and biometric confirmation. Old fashioned, but Thorane's network prefers physical authentication." A pause. "The window opens in eleven hours, Captain. The *Endurance* is a good ship for the Sable Corridor. I checked."

The datapad sits between you.

The data chip from Davan is in your pocket.

Khe'Voz is at the *Endurance* asking a bad capacitor to be honest about its intentions.

Slink is watching sensors and carrying something you haven't named yet.

Davan and Kes are twenty minutes from your ramp.

And MIRA has eleven hours and forty minutes of transit time to do arithmetic that implies opinions.

You press your thumb to the datapad.

The manifest locks. The confirmation chimes. Somewhere in Thorane's network, a pull order begins its journey toward a lien that has had your ship's name on it long enough.

The broker picks up their drink for the first time.

"Safe flying, Captain Richards," they say.

You stand up without answering.

Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — it doesn't work that fast — but something underneath it shifts. The specific relief of a weight that has been carried alone long enough to feel permanent, discovering it can be shared.

He nods. Small. Once.

"Okay," he says. Meaning more than okay.

He's quiet for a moment, and you can see him moving from the emotional register into memory — the deliberate shift of someone trying to be useful now that useful is allowed.

"The voice was filtered," he says. "Not heavily — not the kind of scrambling that makes someone sound like static. More like they were routing through two or three relays. Enough to strip accent and pitch. Could have been anyone." He pauses. "But the cadence was careful. Like someone who chooses words slowly on purpose. Not because they're searching for them — because they want you to hear each one separately."

You file that. The broker speaks the same way.

"Signal ID was spoofed — I checked after the first contact, before I decided not to respond again. It was bouncing off a Gannett relay but the origin point kept moving. Someone with decent network access and the patience to set it up properly."

He pulls his knees closer.

"One thing though." His eyes go to the middle distance — the specific focus of someone retrieving a detail they didn't know mattered until now. "Third contact. I almost didn't pick up. When I didn't respond immediately there was a sound in the background before the message cut." He pauses. "Pressurization cycling. The specific kind — the three-beat pattern. Station-side, not ship-side."

He looks at you.

"I've only heard that pattern in one place on Gannett. The Harbour Office has an old pressure regulator on their administrative ring that cycles like that." He says it carefully, like he's aware of the weight. "I don't know if it means anything. Could be coincidence. Could be someone standing near an exterior access."

He could have buried this. Filed it under *probably nothing* and kept flying quiet.

He didn't.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says softly through the alcove speaker, *"the Harbour Office pressure regulator cycles on a nineteen-minute interval. Station maintenance logs — which are semi-public — show it's been flagged for replacement for eight months. It's somewhat distinctive."*

A beat.

*"I'm also noting that Sura has been in Bay C-19 for one hour and forty-three minutes. The junction box work should have taken twenty minutes. I've been patient about mentioning this but I find my patience has a threshold."*

Slink is watching your face.

"The person who contacted me," he says quietly. "They're connected to what's happening right now, aren't they."

Not a question. Twenty years old and he's already learned to read the shape of bad timing.

"MIRA. Begin undock sequence. We're leaving now."

*"Acknowledged. Clamp release in ninety seconds. Umbilical disconnect on my mark."*

You're moving through the ship as you say it — cockpit first, strapping in, hands finding the familiar worn geography of the pilot controls before the seat is fully warm.

"All crew. We're undocking. Stations."

The *Endurance* responds like it always does — not instantly, not dramatically, but with the settled competence of a ship that has done this enough times to mean it. Systems coming up in sequence. The lighting shifts from umbilical amber to internal power's slightly warmer tone. The hull makes the small sounds it makes when it's waking up properly.

Khe'Voz: *"Engineering nominal. Standing by."*

Slink: *"Sensors live. Bay C-7 still internal — they haven't noticed."*

*"Umbilical disconnecting,"* MIRA says. *"Three. Two. One."*

A thunk through the hull. The last physical connection to Gannett Station releasing. The *Endurance* is on its own fuel now.

*"Clamp release authorized. Releasing in—"*

Your comm chirps. Text message. No sender ID — but you recognize the cadence.

*Captain Richards. Leaving early. Interesting choice. Safe transit.*

The broker.

They're watching the departure board. Of course they are. They know you've unclamped eleven minutes ahead of the filed window. They don't know about Bay C-7's fuel issue. They don't know about the Ossel gap. They don't know about Davan and Kes and the data chip.

But they know you're running.

*"Clamps released,"* MIRA says.

The bay falls away beneath you — or rather the *Endurance* rises, the magnetic clamps disengaging in sequence, the ship lifting free of Gannett's spine on maneuvering thrust so gentle it feels like breathing out. Through the cockpit glass the bay doors open ahead of you, the station's non-rotating spine giving way to the hard black of open space and Gannett's distant star throwing long shadows across the docking infrastructure.

Bay C-19 becomes a rectangle of warm light behind you. Sura is a small orange-jacketed figure next to an open junction panel, watching the *Endurance* go.

You don't look back for long.

"MIRA. Ossel gap deviation. Silent load — primary route on the beacon, actual course on your navigation only."

*"Already running it. We'll diverge from the filed corridor approach at mark seven — approximately forty minutes out. At that point we are off the beacon tracking grid and on drift navigation through the gap."* A pause. *"The unofficial beacon I mentioned. It's pinging right now. Stronger than the last time we transited. Someone has done maintenance on it recently."*

The station shrinks behind you. Gannett's ring catches the starlight, that tired beautiful cylinder of four thousand people improvising civilization out of wreckage and stubbornness.

Toussaint is in their office. Looking at a departure notification that just processed eleven minutes early.

*"Bay C-7,"* Slink reports from sensors, his voice carrying something that might be satisfaction, *"is still in fuel review. No movement. No departure prep."*

Twenty minutes. Maybe forty. The gap between you and Meridian's tail is opening with every kilometer of maneuvering thrust.

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway. Takes in the stars with an expression that has several things in it — relief, calculation, something older and heavier underneath both.

"We're clear of the station," they say.

"For now," you say.

Davan nods. They understand *for now.*

*"Nine hours eleven minutes to waypoint three,"* MIRA says. *"Ossel gap deviation adds ninety minutes. Unmaintained beacon ahead. Something large at waypoint three drawing power on a six hour cycle. Bay C-7 between twenty and forty minutes behind us assuming they clear the fuel review."*

She pauses.

*"We have time to think. I recommend using it."*

The *Endurance* runs clean into the black, Gannett falling away behind it, the Sable Corridor opening ahead like a question that has committed to being answered.

Khe'Voz comes over the crew channel. You can hear him moving toward the galley — the specific sound of a man who processes problems by feeding people.

*"Who wants something hot,"* he says. *"Because we have nine hours and I refuse to face a Meridian logistics platform on an empty stomach."*

result.prose:
The climate management panel is a grey rectangle on the hold's aft bulkhead, between the auxiliary power coupling and a damage control station that has three replacement parts zip-tied to it because the original housing cracked eighteen months ago and nobody's found the right components since.

You've stood in front of this panel a hundred times. Adjusted hold temperature for perishable cargo, for equipment with thermal tolerances, for the occasional passenger who complained about the cold.

Never for this.

The controls are simple. That's the thing that strikes you as you reach for them. Two adjustments. A cutoff toggle and a target temperature input. The kind of interface designed for routine use by people with other things to think about.

Your hand rests on the toggle for exactly one second.

Four thousand people on Verada Station Three.

You cut the cooling.

The panel chimes once — soft, confirmatory, entirely indifferent to what the decision means. The climate regulation unit at the pallet base cycles down with a descending hum, the sound of something that has been working continuously since the containers were loaded finally stopping. The hold settles into a different quality of silence.

*"Climate regulation offline,"* MIRA confirms. *"Hold temperature currently six point two degrees. Target threshold fourteen degrees. Estimated time to threshold: thirty-eight minutes. Compound degradation window opens at threshold. Six hours to biological inertness."*

A beat.

*"Degradation clock starts in thirty-eight minutes. I'll track it."*

You stand there for a moment in the hold's settling silence. The containers look exactly the same as they did thirty seconds ago. Grey. Patient. The Bloc stamps catching the work lighting.

Nothing visible has changed.

Everything has changed.

*"Rix."* MIRA's voice shifts register — the one that means she's moving to something else, something that requires your attention now. *"I've been monitoring Bay C-7's position since departure. I have an updated track."*

You're already moving toward the cockpit.

*"They didn't follow the filed route to waypoint one. They deviated almost immediately after clearing Gannett's approach corridor — which means they either anticipated the Ossel gap deviation or they have a position tracker on the* Endurance *that doesn't rely on beacon reporting."*

The cockpit wraps around you. Nav display live. The Sable Corridor in amber, the Ossel gap deviation plotted silently alongside the filed route. And there — a second track, green, moving with the specific vector of a ship that knows where you're going rather than following where you've been.

*"Current lead: eleven minutes. Closing at approximately forty seconds per hour — their drive is marginally more efficient than ours at this velocity. At current closure rate they intercept our position in approximately sixteen hours."*

*"We reach the Ossel gap in three hours four minutes. Verada Station One in eight hours forty minutes beyond that. They cannot catch us before Verada if we maintain current thrust."*

A pause.

*"However. The platform at waypoint three is on the filed route. Not the Ossel deviation. If they're following us rather than the beacon, they'll take the deviation with us and bypass the platform."*

Another pause. Smaller.

*"Which means the platform won't receive the containers on schedule. And the people running this operation will know something is wrong approximately eight hours before we reach Verada."*

Slink is in the sensor chair. He's been listening, building the picture with the focused quiet that has replaced the withdrawn quiet entirely. He pulls up the C-7 track and overlays it with the platform's last known position and the Ossel deviation vector.

He looks at it for a long moment.

"They have a tracker on us," he says. Not a question. The certainty of someone who has been watching the sensor data and done the math. "Beacon lag doesn't explain that deviation vector. They knew which way we turned before the beacon network could have told them."

He swivels the chair to face you.

"Someone put something on the ship." Flat. Factual. "On Gannett. While we were docked."

The bay. The hours when the *Endurance* sat clamped and accessible. The cargo loader. The dock workers in their orange jackets.

Sura, working the junction box for three hours longer than the job required. Hands that paused near the hull access panel twice during your conversation — you filed it at the time as nervous energy.

It wasn't nervous energy.

*"If there's a physical tracker,"* MIRA says, *"it will be on the hull exterior or in the cargo loading interface — somewhere accessible during the berth window without requiring entry to the ship proper. I can't scan for it internally. Someone would need to go outside."*

Outside. As in EVA. As in the Sable Corridor at current velocity with an eleven minute lead that is closing at forty seconds per hour.

Khe'Voz's voice comes over the crew channel. Steady as always, with the particular quality of a man who has been listening to everything and has formed a complete opinion.

*"I'll go. I know the hull. If something's on her that shouldn't be, I'll find it faster than anyone."*

A beat.

*"Also if I stay in the engine bay thinking about those containers much longer I'm going to need something useful to do with my hands."*

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway. Kes behind them, the data chip back in Davan's jacket, the reader stowed. They've heard enough.

"The platform," Davan says. "When they realize we've bypassed it—"

"They'll know before Verada," you say.

Davan nods. They already knew. They were confirming that you did too.

"Vasek," Kes says. One word. The Remnant inspector at Verada Station One. The half-built case that just found its missing piece. The person who needs to know what's coming before Meridian knows it's coming.

*"I can send a compressed encrypted burst to Verada Station One,"* MIRA says. *"Tight beam, low probability of corridor intercept. The seal data, the compound specifications, BCF-7714, MLC-2291. Everything Khe'Voz scanned and everything from the data chip."*

*"I'd need a contact identifier for Vasek. A comm address, a frequency, something."*

Davan already has their comm out.

The hold is warming toward fourteen degrees.

The degradation clock hasn't started yet.

Vasek is eight hours and forty minutes away.

And somewhere on the *Endurance's* hull, if Slink is right, something is telling Bay C-7 exactly where you are.

Three problems. Simultaneous. The corridor doesn't care about sequencing.

They step forward. Just one step. Enough to close the distance from *professional* to *personal.*

"I'm not asking you what's in the container. I'm telling you it doesn't matter what's in the container. What matters is that you have a crew, a ship you've kept flying through worse than this, and a debt that doesn't care about your principles." A pause. "Thorane doesn't negotiate. You know that. I'm the negotiation."

Sura still hasn't looked up. But the multi-tool has been set down on the deck beside the open panel.

The Pressure Seal earns its name from the low ceiling and the particular density of its atmosphere — bodies, recycled air, something fermented that passes for whiskey at the frontier price point. It's Gannett's neutral ground by long tradition, which means everyone here is pretending not to watch everyone else with professional thoroughness.

The broker is at a corner table. Alone. The analog watch on their wrist, the datapad face-down beside a half-finished drink. They see you come through the door and do not change their expression, which is its own kind of tell.

They were confident you'd come. They're not surprised. But something in the set of their shoulders adjusts — fractional, the way a person recalibrates when reality arrives and matches their model almost perfectly.

*Almost.*

You pull out the chair across from them and sit.

"Captain Richards."

"I'll take the job."

They reach for the datapad. The grey fields on the manifest begin to unlock, one by one, with the soft sequential tones of a document becoming real.

They turn the datapad toward you. "Thorane's representative gets the release authorization the moment you unclamp. Your berth hold dissolves."

The manifest sits between you. Signature line waiting.

The broker looks up.

Something happens in the broker's face. Too fast to name completely. A door closing in a room you weren't supposed to know existed.

"Packaging materials," they say. "Reinforced housing for transit stress on the Sable Corridor. Standard for military-grade components."

The lie is good. It would be good enough for someone who hadn't already heard the truth in a transit lounge forty meters away.

You hold their gaze for one second. Two.

Then you pick up the stylus and sign.

The broker exhales — barely, barely — and takes the datapad back. Their fingers move through the authorization sequence with practiced efficiency. Somewhere across the station, in Thorane's representative's office or comm terminal or whatever room contains the mechanism of your debt, a file updates.

Your comm buzzes.

*Lien status: RESOLVED. Berth hold: RELEASED pending departure authorization. Bay C-19 clamps: AVAILABLE FOR RELEASE.*

*"Rix,"* MIRA says in your earpiece, quiet as a diagnostic reading, *"the marker is cleared. We're free to leave."*

A pause.

*"I want to note, for the record, that 'free' is doing considerable work in that sentence."*

The broker slides a cargo transfer authorization across the table. Twelve containers. Bay C-19. Loading window: two hours.

"The containers will be at your bay within the hour," they say. "Pleasant transit, Captain."

You stand.

"One more thing," the broker says, without looking up from the datapad. The tone has shifted — still professional, but with something underneath it now. Something that was waiting for the signature before it had permission to speak.

"The Sable Corridor has company these days. If you're hailed by anyone claiming Sable Hand affiliation—" they look up "—don't engage. Don't negotiate. Run the corridor hot and don't look back." 

The analog watch catches the bar light.

They know Davan and Kes exist. Maybe not that they're already on your ship. But they know the Hand has been moving.

You keep your face exactly where it is.

"I'll keep that in mind," you say.

And walk out of the Pressure Seal into the concourse with forty thousand credits of cleared debt, twelve containers of colony-killer inbound to Bay C-19, two Sable Hand passengers already boarding your ship, and eleven hours until the transit window opens.

result.prose:
Slink looks up when you say *I believe you.*

Not dramatically. Just — the specific physiological response of someone who has been holding something heavy and has just been told they can put it down. His shoulders drop a fraction. His jaw unclenches. Two months of carried weight, redistributed in about three seconds.

He nods. Once. Taking it in.

"Okay," he says quietly. Like Khe'Voz's *okay* — the word that means more than it says.

Then your second question lands and something shifts in his expression. The shame receding, something more focused moving in behind it. He's been thinking about this. You can see it — the answer was already organized, waiting for someone to ask.

"The signal ID was scrubbed," he says. "Clean scrub, not a cheap one. Whoever they used for that knew what they were doing." He pauses. "But the first contact — the one on Perdis — there was a lag in it. Like the scrub was running half a second behind the transmission. MIRA might be able to do something with that if I give her the timestamp."

He reaches into his jacket pocket and produces a small personal log chip. Holds it out.

"I kept records," he says. "All four contacts. Timestamps, duration, any signal characteristics I could pull manually." A beat. "I didn't know what I was going to do with them. I just — it felt wrong not to."

The chip sits in his palm between you.

"The voice was synthesized," he continues. "But badly synthesized on the third contact — like the modifier clipped for half a second. Underneath it was—" he frowns, reaching for the memory "—I don't know. Station accent. Not Gannett. Something with that flat vowel sound. Verada cluster, maybe. Or somewhere on the Sable Corridor."

He registers his own words landing.

Looks at you.

"That's where we're going," he says. Not a question.

*"Slink,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker, at the gentle volume she uses when she's been listening and has decided to stop pretending otherwise, *"if you give me that chip and the Perdis timestamp, I can run the signal lag against known scrubbing architectures. It won't give us a name. It might give us a network."*

A pause.

*"Also — for what it's worth — I monitored all four contact attempts on the ship's external comm array. I didn't tell Rix because I was waiting to understand what I was looking at."* Another pause, shorter. *"I'm telling both of you now because we're about to fly toward whoever sent them, and that seems like relevant timing."*

Khe'Voz has stopped whistling forward. The repair is done. The *Endurance* hums clean and even.

Slink looks at the chip in his palm. Then at you.

"I want to help fix this," he says. "Whatever this is." The fierce undertone from before, but directed now. Purposeful. "They thought I was the weak link. I want to be the reason that was the wrong call."
