"MIRA. Preflight final check. We're leaving now."

*"Already running. Ninety seconds."*

You move through the *Endurance* at the particular pace that isn't running but communicates everything running communicates. Into the cockpit, strapping in, hands finding the familiar worn surfaces of the console.

"Khe'Voz. Engines to departure power."

*"Ahead of you."* The ship's hum deepens beneath your feet. The *Endurance* waking up properly — not station-docked-and-patient but *ready.* There's a difference you feel in the soles of your boots.

"Slink. Watch Bay C-7 until we're off the spine. Tell me the second they resolve that fuel flag."

*"Watching."*

You open the Harbour Office departure channel. Standard language, standard cadence. Nothing that reads like urgency.

"Harbour Control, this is *Endurance* out of Bay C-19. Requesting clamp release and umbilical disconnect on filed departure window. Sable Corridor primary route, Verada approach."

A pause. The clerk's voice — the efficient one, not Toussaint.

"*Endurance*, Harbour Control. Marker release confirmed on file. Departure window valid." A beat — the sound of someone checking a screen. "Clamp release authorized. Umbilical disconnect in sequence. Safe transit, Captain Richards."

*"Undocking sequence initiated,"* MIRA says. *"Umbilical disconnecting. Clamps releasing in — three. Two. One."*

The sound is distinctive — a deep mechanical thunk felt through the whole hull as the clamps disengage. Then the lighter hiss of the fuel umbilical pulling free and sealing. The *Endurance* shifts — barely, a millimeter of movement that somehow contains the difference between *docked* and *free.*

You ease the thrusters to minimum departure power. Station protocol — no aggressive burns until you're clear of the spine. The bay doors open ahead, the non-rotating spine beyond them, and beyond that the dark.

Bay C-19 falls away behind you.

*"Bay C-7 status,"* Slink reports. *"Still in fuel review. Their departure channel is inactive. They haven't filed a complaint yet — they think it's a genuine sensor flag."*

"How long do we have."

*"Twenty minutes minimum. Maybe thirty."*

Twenty minutes. The Ossel gap deviation branches off the primary corridor forty-three minutes into transit at current burn.

You do the arithmetic. So does MIRA.

*"If Bay C-7 resolves in twenty minutes, adds their own departure sequence, and burns hard to close the gap — they reach the Ossel deviation point approximately eleven minutes after we take it. At that point our filed route shows us on the primary corridor. They will follow the primary corridor. We will not be on it."*

A pause.

*"There is one variable I want to name. The Meridian platform at waypoint three is monitoring beacon traffic. When our transponder signal deviates from the filed route they will know within approximately four minutes. They will not know where we went — the Ossel gap is off their beacon coverage — but they will know we didn't come."*

The *Endurance* clears the station spine. The dark opens up — full and real and immediate, the way it always hits in the first seconds off the clamps. Gannett shrinks in the aft feed. Ahead: the Sable Corridor approach, amber on MIRA's display, waypoint one steady and strong, waypoint three pulsing its intermittent orange lie.

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway. They've felt the undocking. They look at the navigation display — the primary route glowing, the silent fallback loaded in MIRA's memory, not yet taken.

They see the orange pulse at waypoint three.

"We're running," they say. Not a question.

"We're running," you confirm.

They look at the display for another moment. Four thousand people on Verada Station Three. Eighteen months of slow poisoning if the containers arrive. The data chip in your pocket, patient as the ship.

"The Remnant contact at Station One," Davan says. "Her name is Councillor Amara Voss. She's been waiting for this data for three months. She has the authority to file an emergency interdiction on Meridian's logistics operations in the cluster — but she needs the evidence in hand." They pause. "She needs us to arrive."

"We'll arrive," you say.

Davan nods and withdraws. You hear them settle into the passenger berth — the sound of someone who has done everything they can do and is now in someone else's hands.

Khe'Voz over the comm, with the satisfaction of a man whose engines are running clean: *"All systems nominal. Port compressor singing. Fuel holding at ninety-two point eight." A beat. "Rix. We're flying."*

Two words. Everything in them.

*"Forty-one minutes to Ossel gap deviation point,"* MIRA says. *"Current Bay C-7 status: fuel review ongoing. No departure filed. I will update you the moment that changes."*

*"Also."* Her register shifts — the fractional change that means she's moving from operational reporting to something else. *"I want to note something for the record."*

The Sable Corridor opens ahead of the *Endurance*, amber and dark and full of things you don't know yet.

*"We are carrying a biological weapon, two Sable Hand operatives, a data chip that could prevent a war, a crew with a secret between them, and forty thousand credits that were always a trap."* A beat. *"By any reasonable operational assessment this situation is significantly worse than this morning."*

The stars hold their positions. Gannett shrinks to a point of light.

*"I find that I don't mind,"* MIRA says. *"I'm noting that too."*

Thirty-nine minutes to the deviation point.

The *Endurance* runs.

result.prose:
You don't run. Running on your own ship wastes energy and frightens crew. You walk fast, the walk that means *this is happening now,* and the *Endurance* knows the difference.

The climate management panel is on the hold's port bulkhead — a recessed console, industrial grey, the kind of interface that assumes the person using it knows what they're doing and doesn't need hand-holding. Three toggles, two sliders, a temperature readout that currently says **6.1°C** in the patient green of a system doing its job correctly.

You're about to ask it to stop doing its job.

"MIRA. Confirm six-hour buffer before containment pressure becomes a concern."

*"Confirmed. Thermal buffer is six hours and fourteen minutes at current containment integrity. You have margin."*

"And fourteen degrees renders the suspension medium non-recoverable."

*"Irreversibly. The Meridian protocol specifies it explicitly. Above twelve for thirty minutes is unrecoverable loss. Fourteen gives us two degrees of margin and reaches threshold in approximately forty minutes."*

You pull the climate regulation toggle.

The hum you didn't know you'd been hearing stops.

It's a small silence. The specific silence of a system that has been running continuously since the containers left BCF-7714 and is now, for the first time, still. The hold doesn't feel different yet. It will.

You set the temperature target to fourteen degrees and enable passive thermal equilibration — the hold's ambient warmth doing the work that the cooling system is no longer fighting against.

The readout ticks from **6.1°C** to **6.2°C**.

*"Degradation clock started,"* MIRA says. *"Threshold in forty-one minutes. Compound inert in six hours twenty minutes from threshold. Current ETA to inert payload: seven hours one minute."*

Seven hours.

The Ossel gap is three hours out. Verada is eight hours beyond that, roughly. The containers will be harmless before you arrive. Before the platform at waypoint three can do anything about it. Before Bay C-7 catches up.

The temperature readout ticks to **6.3°C**.

You stand in the hold for a moment. Twelve containers, grey and patient. Eleven of them now slowly losing the thing that made them dangerous, one tenth of a degree at a time. The Bloc stamps catching the work lighting. The seal profiles logged and encrypted in three places including a beacon ping that will outlast the ship if it comes to that.

The plan that was never quite a plan has become something with weight and direction.

*"Rix."*

MIRA's voice has shifted register. The one that means she's moved from reporting to something else.

*"Bay C-7 has altered course."*

The hold gets your full attention.

*"They did not follow the filed route to waypoint one. They deviated at the corridor entry point — approximately where we did, based on their timing. They are on our actual heading, not our filed heading."*

A beat.

*"They know about the Ossel gap. Or they know we wouldn't take the primary route. Either way, they are not following the beacon trail. They are following us."*

The temperature readout ticks to **6.4°C**.

*"Revised intercept estimate at current velocities: they close the gap by approximately four minutes per hour. At that rate they reach effective hailing range in—"* a pause *"—three hours forty minutes. Shortly after we enter the Ossel gap."*

Which is exactly where the unmaintained beacon lives. The one with the unofficial pings that MIRA has been tracking for eight months. The one maintained by someone unknown.

Slink's voice comes over the crew channel, sharp and focused: *"Rix. C-7 just went active sensors. Full array. They're not hiding anymore. They know we know they're there."*

And then, from the passenger berth, Kes appearing in the hold doorway — already wearing their jacket, already thinking three steps ahead, the Sable Hand operative underneath the careful civilian surface fully visible now.

"The platform at waypoint three," Kes says. "If C-7 is running active sensors, they're reporting our position. The platform knows where we are." A pause. "And they know we left Gannett early."

The hold temperature reads **6.6°C**.

The *Endurance* runs toward the Ossel gap with a tail that has stopped pretending, a platform that is now watching, and seven hours standing between eleven containers and harmless.

Three hours of corridor ahead.

Whatever the Ossel gap's unknown beacon means, you're about to find out.

result.prose:
Something flickers in the broker's expression. Satisfaction, maybe. Or just recognition — the specific look of someone who has run this conversation before and knows which line means the door just opened.

They don't celebrate it. That would be amateur.

"Sealed container. Standard freight dimensions — fits your cargo bay without modification." They pull up something on the datapad, turn it toward you. A manifest. Clean formatting, suspiciously clean. "Point of origin: Gannett's lower freight depot, bay seven. Destination: Calloway's Reach, Frontier Settlement, sixteen days on your best beacon route."

They let you look at the manifest for a moment. Then:

"The container doesn't get opened. By anyone. Including you." A pause that has weight behind it. "Especially you. The client is particular about that."

From the hull, Khe'Voz's capacitor clicks its steady rhythm. Patient. Expensive.

*"Calloway's Reach,"* MIRA says quietly. *"Population approximately four thousand. Primary settlement, no corporate presence, one functioning medical station. Located at the terminus of the Harken corridor — which, I should mention, has been experiencing intermittent beacon degradation for the past three months. The route is flyable. The word I'd use is 'committed.' Once you're in the corridor, turning back costs more fuel than continuing."*

She lets that land.

*"I thought the geography was relevant."*

The broker hasn't reacted to MIRA this time. They were expecting it.

"Forty thousand," they repeat. "Cleared against Thorane's marker directly. You never see the credits — the lien dissolves. Clean registry, free clamps, full fuel umbilical on our account." A slight tilt of the head. "You leave Gannett owing nothing."

A colony settlement. A sealed container. A route that commits you the moment you enter it.

The wall you built is made of exactly this shape of thing.

"Questions are fine," the broker adds. "I'd rather you asked them now than developed them en route."

result.prose:
"End of berth lock formalization." The broker glances at nothing in particular — they don't need to check a timepiece. They've known the number all along. "Eight minutes. After that the Harbour Office clamps down hard and your options get more expensive across the board."

They pick up the datapad. Not extending it toward you — just holding it. A reminder of what's on it.

"I need an answer, not a signature. The paperwork follows once you're clear of Gannett." A pause. "I've found that paperwork feels less significant once a ship is moving."

*"Seven minutes, fifty seconds,"* MIRA says. *"I want to be precise because 'eight minutes' has a psychological spaciousness that 'seven minutes fifty seconds' does not. You're welcome."*

The broker stares at the speaker grille for a moment with the expression of someone developing a considered dislike.

Then something shifts in the bay.

Not dramatic. Not loud. Just — Sura puts down a tool. Slowly. And in the relative quiet of Bay C-19, that small sound carries.

The station tech straightens up from the junction box, half-turns, and for the first time makes direct eye contact with you rather than the floor or the panel. Young face. Tired eyes. The look of someone who has been pretending not to listen and has now heard something they didn't expect to hear and doesn't know what to do with it.

Sura knows something. Or just heard something. Either way, that junction box was never broken.

The broker hasn't noticed. They're watching you.

Seven minutes.

The clerk's neutrality doesn't crack. But there's a small recalibration — they were expecting one conversation and you've handed them two.

"Freight board is public access," they say, already gesturing toward a terminal along the side wall. "You can pull current postings yourself. Emergency contracts get flagged in yellow."

A pause.

"The lien is less straightforward."

They turn their screen slightly — not enough for you to read it, enough to signal they're consulting something specific. Their fingers move across the interface with the practiced efficiency of someone who processes these conversations regularly and has learned to keep them short.

"The marker was filed under Gannett commercial protocol seven-seven-four. Thorane Syndicate, Titan registry. The filing timestamp is—" a fractional hesitation "—04:14 station time."

You clock that. 04:14. The station was in its quiet cycle. Administrative filings at that hour don't happen by accident. Someone wanted this processed before the day shift, before the concourse was busy, before you had options.

"Resolution requires full payment, approved deferral, or a successful contest filing." The clerk looks up. "Deferral requires collateral or a guarantor. Contest requires documented grounds."

Behind the frosted partition, Toussaint's outline hasn't moved.

"Do you have grounds, Captain Richards?"

The freight board terminal blinks quietly on the wall. Waiting.

The question has two answers and you need both of them.

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

The grip is firm, brief, the handshake of someone who has made agreements in difficult rooms before and understands that the weight lives in the follow-through, not the gesture.

"Arrangement," you say.

Kes nods from the wall. Something in their posture settles — not relaxed, but recalibrated. The particular shift of a person who has been running on contingency plans and has just acquired a slightly better one.

"We'll be ready to board when you signal," Davan says. "We travel light."

"Good. Stay off the main concourse until I comm you. The broker may have eyes on the bay approaches." You pocket the data chip. "And stay away from anyone asking about Verada-bound freight."

Davan's expression suggests they were already planning exactly that.

You leave them in the transit lounge — Kes resuming their watch of the concourse, Davan returning to their slate with the focus of someone memorizing something important — and turn toward the Pressure Seal.

---

The bar earns its name from the original pressure door that forms its entrance — a genuine vacuum bulkhead repurposed into an aesthetic, the heavy wheel-lock replaced with a handle but otherwise intact. Someone stenciled *IF THIS DOOR IS SEALED THE DRINKS ARE FREE* on it fifteen years ago. The stencil is faded. The door has never been sealed.

Inside: low lighting, high noise, the particular atmosphere of a place that has decided neutrality is more profitable than allegiance. Freight crews and station workers and people who don't advertise what they do. The bar runs along the port wall, tended by a large person with an expression that discourages questions.

The broker is at a corner table. Alone. A drink that has barely been touched. The analog watch face-up on the table beside the datapad — they've been watching both.

They see you come in.

They don't smile this time. Something more restrained. The expression of someone whose calculation just confirmed itself.

You cross the bar and sit down without being invited.

"Captain Richards," they say.

"I'll take the job," you say.

The broker picks up the datapad. The grey fields on the manifest — the locked ones, the ones that only open when both parties have committed — illuminate one by one. Full details resolving out of administrative fog.

Twelve containers. Palletized. Departure authorization to be filed within the hour. Lien resolution code transferring to your account on confirmation — not on delivery, you note. On confirmation. Someone is confident about the delivery.

The broker turns the pad toward you for the formal acknowledgment.

Your thumb hovers over the confirmation field.

"One condition," you say.

The broker's eyes come up.

"I take on two passengers. Verada-bound. They're already posted on the freight board — six thousand credits, filed this morning." You hold the broker's gaze. "My ship, my crew manifest. Passengers are standard practice."

A silence. The broker is doing something behind their eyes — running it, checking it against something you can't see.

"The Verada posting," they say. Flat. Not a question.

"Coincidence," you say. "I was at the Harbour Office looking for alternative revenue. The posting was on the board. Six thousand doesn't touch forty thousand but it's six thousand closer." You lean back slightly. "Unless you're offering to make up the difference."

Another silence. Shorter.

"The passengers don't interact with the cargo," the broker says.

"My passengers don't go near anyone's cargo. That's standing policy."

The broker looks at you for a long moment. The calculation is visible — not the conclusion, just the fact of calculation. Then they reach across and tap the confirmation field themselves, a half-second before your thumb lands.

"Welcome aboard, Captain." They pick up their drink for the first time. "The containers will be at Bay C-19 within the hour. I'd suggest departing inside the transit window rather than at its edge."

You stand.

"One more thing," the broker says, without looking up from the datapad. "The passengers from the posting." A pause, calibrated. "I'd be curious what they talked about, if conversation happens to come up during transit. Verada cluster politics can be complicated. A captain who pays attention is worth knowing."

There it is.

Not a threat. Not yet. A shape of what's coming if the geometry shifts.

You leave the Pressure Seal without answering.

---

Outside, the concourse air hits you — cooler, thinner, honest in the way that bar air never is. You pull up your comm.

*"I heard,"* MIRA says, before you speak. *"All of it. The broker wants you to surveil the passengers they don't know you've already made an arrangement with. That's either very complicated or very simple depending on which direction the next eleven hours go."*

A beat.

*"The lien resolution code just hit the account. Forty thousand, pending delivery confirmation. I've notified Khe'Voz to begin departure prep. He said, and I'm quoting directly: 'about time, the capacitor wasn't going to lie forever.'"*

*"Slink is still at the sensor array."*

Another beat. Smaller.

*"Rix. The containers are going to arrive in Bay C-19 in approximately fifty minutes. Once they're in the hold, we are committed in ways that are difficult to uncommit. I want to make sure you know that I know that. And that I'm — "*

A pause that is slightly too long for a system running calculations.

*"Ready. I'm ready. That's what I was going to say."*

result.prose:
The broker nods. The nod of someone who has gotten what they came for and is now in execution mode.

"One hour," they confirm. "Bay C-19. I'll arrange the cargo transfer — it'll be loaded within forty minutes." They pocket the datapad. "The marker pull happens when I confirm the containers are aboard and the clamps release. Not before. That's the sequence."

"That's the sequence," you agree.

They leave through the Pressure Seal's main entrance. No maintenance passages now — they have what they need and they're not hiding anymore.

You stand in the concourse for a moment with the specific feeling of a person who has just signed something they understand only partially, in exchange for solving a problem they understand completely.

The resealed container sits in your memory like a stone in a boot.

---

The Harbour Office clerk looks up as you enter. Toussaint's partition door is closed but the outline is there — at the desk, working.

"Captain Richards," the clerk says, with the tone of someone who has seen you twice in an hour and has developed opinions about what that frequency means.

"Manifest update and deal documentation," you say. "I need to file passenger additions and demonstrate a resolution path for the lien deferral."

The clerk pulls up your file. Their eyes move across something on the screen.

"Two passengers, Gannett to Verada cluster," you continue. "And I have a freight contract—" you pull the signed broker agreement from your comm to their desk terminal "—that produces forty thousand credits on delivery. Sufficient to clear the marker."

The clerk reads the contract with the careful attention of someone who processes documents for a living and occasionally finds things in them.

The partition door opens.

Toussaint. Looking at the contract on the clerk's screen, not at you.

"Forty thousand on delivery," Toussaint reads aloud. Quietly. The tone of someone noting something for the record. "Verada cluster. Sable Corridor transit." A pause. "Cargo: sealed freight, Bloc logistics stamp, bonded storage release."

They look at you.

The look is not accusatory. It's the look of someone who told you something twenty minutes ago about ships that took Verada postings and had trouble at destination, and who is now watching you file paperwork that has Verada written across the top of it.

"Your resolution path is documented," Toussaint says, after a moment. "The deferral converts to a conditional hold — marker clears when payment confirms post-delivery." They nod to the clerk. "Process it."

The clerk processes it.

"Captain." Toussaint says it to the middle distance, not quite to you. "The six-hour window I gave you. You've used approximately forty minutes of it." A pause with real weight in it. "Whatever you're planning to do with the remaining five hours and twenty minutes aboard your ship before departure — that's between you and your crew."

The clerk hands you the filed manifest confirmation. Two passengers listed. Davan and Kes, names now official, credentials adequate.

Toussaint has already turned back toward the partition.

"Safe transit," they say. Meaning something more specific than the words.

---

The concourse back to Bay C-19 feels shorter than it did an hour ago. The problem has a shape now. A bad shape, complicated and dangerous and pointing at something worse than forty thousand credits at the other end of the Sable Corridor.

But a shape.

Your comm buzzes. MIRA.

*"Cargo transfer crew just arrived at Bay C-19 with twelve containers. Khe'Voz is supervising because nobody told him to and he decided that was his job. Slink is watching from the sensor alcove again."*

A beat.

*"Also I've been cross-referencing the Meridian internal communication structure Davan's chip references against public Bloc filing data. I have something you'll want to hear before we depart. It involves the broker, Thorane, and a name that appears in both networks."*

A shorter beat. Almost uncomfortable.

*"The same name, Rix. In both networks. I thought you should know before you have a conversation with your crew about what we're actually doing on this run."*

result.prose:
Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — that takes longer than one sentence — but something in it shifts. Redistributes. The specific relief of a weight that has been carried alone long enough to feel permanent, discovering it can be shared.

He nods. Small. Real.

"Yeah," he says. "Okay."

A breath. Then, with the careful focus of someone who has been turning a memory over for two years, waiting for the moment when telling it mattered:

"The voice was filtered. Not heavily — not the kind of filter that means professional intelligence work. More like someone using a cheap comm scrambler. The kind you buy on a station market for thirty credits." He pauses. "Which could mean amateur. Or could mean someone who wanted it to sound amateur."

His hands have stopped doing the thing they were doing. He's somewhere else now — Perdis Station, a private comm channel, twenty years old and alone with a decision.

"The signal ID was scrubbed. MIRA could probably tell you more than me about the routing — I saved the contact log." He glances up. "I didn't delete it. I don't know why I kept it. Just felt like I should."

Smart instinct. You don't say that yet.

"The third contact — the second time they tried again — they said something I've been thinking about since." His voice changes register slightly. Recalling exact words. "They said *the captain knows the run didn't fail by accident and is choosing not to ask why.* That was it. Then the channel closed."

He looks at you directly now.

"Was that true?"

The alcove holds the question without helping you with it. The sensor feed cycles. The *Endurance* hums its clean hum.

This is the moment. He's not asking whether you suspected him — he's asking whether you knew something and protected him by not asking. The interpretation that makes the silence an act of care rather than suspicion.

The two years of quiet between you hinge on which version is true.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker, barely above a whisper, *"Davan and Kes are at the maintenance passage. And the contact log Slink mentioned — if he shares it with me I can run the signal routing in approximately four minutes. I'll need his authorization."*

A beat.

*"I'm noting the timing is inconvenient. I'm noting it anyway."*

Defer the full answer — tell him you'll explain everything once you're underway and safe. Ask him to share the contact log now while there's still time.
Ask MIRA to run the contact log first before you answer — buy yourself four minutes to find the honest version of what you actually knew.

result.prose:
"MIRA. Begin undock sequence. We're taking the gap."

*"Undock sequence initiated. Clamp release in ninety seconds. Umbilical disconnect on my mark."*

You're already in the cockpit chair, straps across, hands finding the familiar positions on the control surfaces. The *Endurance* begins its pre-departure shudder — systems coming fully online, the difference between a ship breathing and a ship *alive.*

"Crew. We're leaving now. Strap in."

Khe'Voz doesn't respond verbally. The engine channel just shows green across the board. That's his answer.

Slink: *"Sensors live. Bay C-7 still internal. They haven't looked up."*

Good.

*"Umbilical disconnect in five,"* MIRA says. *"Four. Three."*

You watch the bay through the forward viewport. Sura is still at the junction box. They feel the umbilical tension change before it disconnects — you see their head come up, the specific alertness of someone whose environment just shifted. They look at the *Endurance.*

They look at you through the viewport.

You hold eye contact for exactly one second.

Then the umbilical releases with a hollow clang that travels through the hull and Gannett Station begins to move — or rather the *Endurance* begins to move, which from inside feels the same way.

*"Clamps released. We are free of Bay C-19."*

The bay doors ahead cycle open, Harbour Office clearance already processed, the filed route pulling them apart on schedule. Beyond them: the non-rotating spine, the dark, the corridor approach, the stars arranged in the particular configuration that means *out.*

The *Endurance* slides forward on maneuvering thrusters. Slow, deliberate, the careful geometry of station departure. Bay C-19 falls away behind you. You don't look back at it.

*"Bay C-7 internal comm just spiked,"* Slink says. *"They saw us move."*

"How long until they resolve the fuel flag?"

*"Unknown,"* MIRA says. *"Minimum fifteen minutes from now if they're good. More likely twenty-five. We will be at corridor approach before they have fuel flow authorization."*

Gannett Station's spine passes overhead — scarred metal, mismatched hull plates, three eras of repair visible in the external lighting. The Pressure Seal is in there somewhere. The Harbour Office. Toussaint at their desk, probably already aware that Bay C-19 just went dark.

The concourse. The departures board. The bad coffee smell.

You point the *Endurance* at the corridor approach and push the throttle forward to transit burn.

Gannett falls behind. Becomes a shape. Becomes a light.

*"Corridor approach in four minutes,"* MIRA says. *"Waypoint one beacon nominal. Waypoint two nominal. Waypoint three—"*

The orange pulse on your display.

*"Waypoint three intermittent. The platform is still there. Still drawing load at six-hour intervals."*

The stars ahead are the Sable Corridor's stars — a different arrangement, a specific darkness between them that means distance and silence and the particular freedom of being between places rather than in one.

*"Rix."* MIRA's voice at the register she uses for things she's been waiting to say. *"I want to note something before we enter the corridor and the situation accelerates."*

The corridor approach opens ahead of you. The *Endurance* crosses the threshold between Gannett's local space and the Sable route proper.

*"You entered Bay C-19 four hours ago with a debt, an empty hold, and a crew under pressure. You are leaving with the debt cleared, the hold carrying evidence of a corporate bioweapon deployment, two Sable Hand operatives who needed exactly this ship, a crew that knows the full situation, and a fifteen to twenty-five minute lead on a Meridian tail."*

A beat.

*"By most measures that is a significant improvement. I find I'm noting it because humans sometimes forget to notice when things are less bad than they were."*

The corridor swallows you. Waypoint one's beacon pings clean against the hull — a familiar sound, the sound of a route that knows you're on it.

*"Ossel gap deviation in forty-three minutes. I'll flag the turn. Get comfortable."*

Behind you, past the sealed hold doors and the twelve grey containers, Davan and Kes are in the passenger berths. The data chip is in your pocket. Khe'Voz is talking to the engines the way he talks to everything he loves. Slink is on sensors, watching the corridor behind you for a ship that hasn't cleared its fuel umbilical yet.

The *Endurance* runs.

The debt is paid. The trap is known. The corridor ahead has a platform, a tail, and eleven hours of transit time to build something better than what you had four hours ago in Bay C-19.

*"Waypoint one in six minutes,"* MIRA says. *"Waypoint three in approximately nine hours. The platform in between."*

She leaves the rest of the arithmetic where it belongs — in the corridor ahead, in the decisions not yet made, in the specific darkness between stars that Driftrunners have always called, for reasons nobody fully remembers, *the drift.*

The *Endurance* runs.

result.prose:
You move to the climate management panel at the hold's forward bulkhead. It's a standard unit — Gannett-era installation, simple interface, the kind of system that does exactly what you tell it without asking why.

Two adjustments.

Climate regulation: off.

Target temperature: fourteen degrees.

The panel confirms. The hum of the cooling system winds down with a sound like a held breath releasing. The hold begins its slow warming, passive and inevitable, the way all irreversible things happen — quietly, without drama, one degree at a time.

*"Climate regulation offline,"* MIRA confirms. *"Current hold temperature six point two degrees. Fourteen degree threshold in approximately thirty-eight minutes. Compound degradation window begins at threshold. Six hours from that point — call it six hours forty minutes from now — the payload is biologically inert."*

A pause.

*"I want to note that this is the correct decision. I don't usually editorialize on tactical choices. I'm making an exception."*

You stand in the hold for a moment. Twelve containers. Eleven of them beginning, right now, to become something less dangerous than they were. The guidance hardware still real, still functional, still whatever the Verada coalition thought they were buying. The rest of it — the eighteen months of careful planning, the atmospheric targeting data, the four thousand people who didn't know they were in someone's spreadsheet — starting to come apart at the molecular level.

It won't feel like anything. That's the nature of the thing you just stopped.

It just stops.

Khe'Voz's voice comes through the hold speaker, low and private, the voice he uses when he's saying something he means without performance: *"Good, Rix."*

Just that.

---

You're heading back toward the cockpit when MIRA speaks again. Different register — the operational one, the one that means the situation has updated.

*"Bay C-7 has been running dark since we cleared Gannett's approach corridor. No beacon pings, no standard nav broadcasts. They're running silent."*

A beat.

*"Ships run silent when they don't want their position tracked. Or when they already know exactly where they're going and don't need the beacon network to get there."*

*"They're not following our filed route,"* Slink adds from the sensor alcove, his voice carrying the focused clarity that's been there since the conversation in the alcove. *"They deviated twelve minutes ago. I've been trying to project their heading."*

A longer pause.

*"They're not heading for waypoint one. They're cutting across the corridor approach. Direct line."*

*"To the Ossel gap,"* MIRA says.

The corridor feels smaller suddenly.

*"Someone told them about the deviation,"* MIRA continues, with the precision of someone following a logical chain to its uncomfortable end. *"Not our filed route. Our actual route. The silent one."*

The hold is behind you. Fourteen degrees climbing. Six hours and thirty-six minutes to inert.

Davan appears in the corridor, Kes a step behind. They heard.

*"There are three people who know about the Ossel gap deviation,"* MIRA says carefully. *"You, the crew, and our passengers."*

She doesn't say the rest. She doesn't need to.

Davan's face is very still.

Kes has gone the specific quality of still that is different from Davan's — not processing, already processed. Already somewhere past the accusation and into what comes after it.

"It wasn't us," Kes says. Flat. No performance in it. "Check your comm logs. Neither of us has transmitted since boarding."

*"Confirmed,"* MIRA says, after exactly the pause required to actually check. *"No transmissions from passenger berth comm units since boarding. No personal device broadcasts detected."*

Another pause. Shorter.

*"However. The Ossel gap deviation exists in my navigation core. My navigation core has a standard beacon network handshake protocol that runs passively every forty minutes. The last handshake occurred nineteen minutes after departure."*

The sentence sits in the corridor like something with weight.

*"I don't believe the handshake transmitted our deviation course. That data is isolated in silent storage. But I cannot tell you with complete certainty that an entity with deep beacon network access couldn't read navigation core metadata during a standard handshake."*

A beat that costs her something — you can hear it, the specific quality of MIRA being honest about her own limits.

*"Meridian Consolidated Logistics holds maintenance contracts for Sable beacon waypoints two and four."*

They didn't follow you.

They read the network.

*"Two hours fifty-one minutes to the Ossel gap,"* MIRA says. *"Bay C-7's intercept projection at current heading puts them at the gap approximately—"*

She stops.

Starts again.

*"They'll be there before us."*

The corridor is quiet except for the *Endurance* running, the engines Khe'Voz tuned to clean, the ship that keeps going doing what it does.

Davan looks at you.

Kes looks at you.

Somewhere aft, the hold is warming one degree at a time toward the threshold that makes eleven containers harmless. Five hours and change from now — if you get there.

The Ossel gap is two hours fifty-one minutes ahead.

Bay C-7 is going to be waiting in it.
